generated_oml:
// C2: Acting Components
instance MotorActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Mechanical actuator component of MABI MAX 100 robot, comprising motor-gearbox assemblies responsible for joint actuation and torque generation. Implicitly referenced in friction identification context."
]

instance GearboxAssembly : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Transmission component integrated with motor actuators in MABI MAX 100 robot, responsible for speed/torque conversion and mechanical power transmission. Critical for friction analysis in robotic joints."
]

// C3: Physical sensing components
instance JointTorqueSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Torque sensor embedded in robotic joint assembly to measure real-time joint torque values for friction identification purposes."
    DTDFVocab:producedData JointTorqueDataTransmitted
]

instance AngularVelocitySensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Encoder-based sensor measuring angular velocity of robotic joints to support dynamic behavior analysis and friction detection."
    DTDFVocab:producedData AngularVelocityDataTransmitted
]

instance OperationalLogSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "System operational logging sensor capturing robot operational metrics and status events for contextual data aggregation."
    DTDFVocab:producedData OperationalLogDataTransmitted
]

// C4: Physical-to-virtual interaction
instance JointTorqueDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom JointTorqueSensor
]

instance AngularVelocityDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom AngularVelocitySensor
]

instance OperationalLogDataTransmitted : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom OperationalLogSensor
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance SysMLInternalBlockDiagram : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Architectural model created using SysML Internal Block Diagrams representing component-connector structure of MABI MAX 100 robot system."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SysMLEnabler
]

instance MontiArcAutomatonModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Behavioral model implemented using MontiArc Automaton framework to describe state transitions and dynamic behavior of robotic actuators and joints."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MontiArcEnabler
]

instance SimulinkFrictionModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Dynamic simulation model created in MathWorks Simulink to model friction characteristics in motor-gearbox systems of MABI MAX 100 robot."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulinkEnabler
]

instance FrictionDataAggregation : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated time-series data representing friction coefficients and wear indicators derived from raw sensor measurements through abstraction processes."
    DTDFVocab:fromData JointTorqueDataTransmitted, AngularVelocityDataTransmitted
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataAggregationEnabler
]

instance MechanicalHealthMetrics : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Processed mechanical health metrics including wear indicators and friction anomaly flags generated from contextual data traces."
    DTDFVocab:fromData OperationalLogDataTransmitted
    DTDFVocab:inputTo VisualizationEnabler
]

// C11: Enablers
instance SysMLEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "SysML toolchain enabling architectural modeling and component-connector validation for MABI MAX 100 robot system."
    DTDFVocab:enables MechanicalHealthMonitoringService
]

instance MontiArcEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MontiArc framework enabling state machine-based behavioral modeling of robotic actuator dynamics and friction analysis."
    DTDFVocab:enables MechanicalHealthMonitoringService
]

instance SimulinkEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink environment enabling dynamic simulation of friction characteristics in motor-gearbox systems."
    DTDFVocab:enables MechanicalHealthMonitoringService
]

instance DataAggregationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MBSE-based data aggregation framework implementing abstraction mechanisms to convert raw sensor data into mechanically meaningful metrics."
    DTDFVocab:enables MechanicalHealthMonitoringService
]

instance VisualizationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Dashboard visualization tool enabling presentation of aggregated friction data and mechanical health metrics to engineering stakeholders."
    DTDFVocab:enables MechanicalHealthMonitoringService
]

instance MQTTProtocol : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MQTT protocol implementation enabling lightweight, automated one-way data transmission from physical sensors to digital shadow representation."
    DTDFVocab:enables DataIngestionService
]

// C6: Services
instance MechanicalHealthMonitoringService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Primary service providing passive monitoring and data aggregation of MABI MAX 100 robot's mechanical health through friction identification and wear analysis."
    DTDFVocab:provides FrictionAnomalyInsight, WearTrendInsight, MechanicalHealthDashboardAction
]

instance DataIngestionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service responsible for automated ingestion of time-series data from physical sensors into digital representation using MQTT protocol."
    DTDFVocab:provides DataCollectionInsight
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance FrictionAnomalyInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Identified friction anomalies in robotic joints derived from processed torque and velocity data through simulation and state machine analysis."
]

instance WearTrendInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Longitudinal analysis of wear trends in motor-gearbox assemblies based on aggregated operational data and simulation results."
]

instance MechanicalHealthDashboardAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Visual presentation of mechanical health metrics and friction analysis results through interactive dashboard interface."
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic false
]

instance DataCollectionInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Verification of successful data collection from physical sensors to digital shadow representation with validation of transmission integrity."
]

// C1: Environment
instance ManufacturingEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:desc "Industrial manufacturing environment where MABI MAX 100 robot operates, characterized by precision assembly tasks and real-time monitoring requirements."
    base:contains MABIMAX100RobotSystem
]

// C5: Components
instance MABIMAX100RobotSystem : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Serial robot MABI MAX 100 designed for precision manufacturing tasks, featuring articulated joints with motor-gearbox assemblies for multi-axis motion control."
    base:isContainedIn ManufacturingEnvironment
]

instance FrictionShadow : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Digital Shadow instance representing the friction identification subsystem of MABI MAX 100 robot, aggregating sensor data for mechanical health analysis."
    base:isContainedIn ManufacturingEnvironment
    base:contains MotorActuator, GearboxAssembly, JointTorqueSensor, AngularVelocitySensor, OperationalLogSensor, SysMLInternalBlockDiagram, MontiArcAutomatonModel, SimulinkFrictionModel, FrictionDataAggregation, MechanicalHealthMetrics
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system's temporal aspects are governed by the synchronization requirements between physical and digital components, which are not explicitly quantified in the provided documents. However, the context implies that **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (e.g., SysML, ADLs like AADL, or MontiArc) are used to model system behavior at varying abstraction levels, including functional and logical architectures. These models often integrate with **simulation tools** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, other simulation methods [29, 30]) and general-purpose languages (e.g., C++, Java) to enforce temporal granularity and latency constraints. The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Fig. 1) suggests a one-way data flow from physical objects to digital representations, implying that synchronization frequencies and latency requirements are implicitly managed by the **active components** (e.g., software systems) interpreting incoming data streams. No specific time scales (e.g., real-time, near-real-time, or batch processing) or synchronization frequencies (e.g., Hz, ms, or s intervals) are detailed in the documents, but the integration of **state machines** (SysML v1.7) and **automata** (MontiArc) indicates discrete-event or event-driven temporal modeling is applicable. Latency requirements are likely derived from the **purpose-specific aggregation and abstraction** of contextual data traces, as described for Digital Shadows, though exact values remain unspecified."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system's **orchestration and coordination** are framed within a **heterogeneous, multi-tool architecture** that integrates **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies with simulation and programming tools. The **architecture patterns** emerge from the use of **ADLs (e.g., AADL, MontiArc)** and **SysML**, which define **component-connector systems** with explicit interfaces and interconnections. **Resource management** is implicitly handled through the **layered decomposition** of system models (e.g., RFLP), where each layer (requirements, functions, logic, physics) operates with scoped responsibilities. **System-wide coordination** is achieved via **state machines** (SysML v1.7) and **automata** (MontiArc), enabling discrete-event-driven behavior and synchronization across components. The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Fig. 1) further suggests that **active components** (e.g., software systems) interpret and process data flows, implying a **reactive orchestration** mechanism. However, the documents do not detail **explicit constellation patterns** (e.g., microservices, service-oriented architectures) or **orchestration frameworks** (e.g., Kubernetes, Docker Swarm). Instead, coordination relies on **tool-specific integration** (e.g., Simulink models linked to C++/Java code) and **shared data models** (e.g., SysML diagrams) to ensure consistency across heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) system in this context is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, encompassing multiple abstraction layers: **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. The lifecycle stages are governed by methodologies such as **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, and **SMArDT**, which systematically decompose system complexity.

**Development stages** include:
1. **Requirements Phase**: Defines system objectives and constraints using structured modeling languages.
2. **Functional Architecture**: Describes system behavior via **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **ACME**, **AADL**, or **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**, focusing on **component-connector perspectives** (e.g., interfaces, interconnections, and composition).
3. **Logical Architecture**: Refines functional models with **state machines (SysML v1.7)** or **MontiArc Automaton** for behavioral granularity.
4. **Product/Technical Layer**: Integrates **simulation tools (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, general-purpose languages like C++/Java)** for detailed behavioral modeling.

**Representation types** evolve from **abstract models (requirements/functional)** to **executable simulations (logical/technical)**. **Transition management** between stages relies on **heterogeneous tool integration** (e.g., SysML + Simulink) and **stakeholder-specific perspectives** (e.g., engineering vs. operational views). **Lifecycle governance** is implicit in MBSE frameworks, emphasizing **iterative refinement** and **cross-domain consistency** (e.g., aligning physical and digital representations). No explicit governance frameworks (e.g., ISO/IEC standards) are mentioned, but **methodological rigor** (e.g., RFLP) ensures traceability across stages.
"
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system’s **fidelity** is implicitly governed by **MBSE abstraction layers (RFLP, OOSEM)** to manage complexity, though explicit **fidelity requirements** (e.g., error tolerances, model granularity) are not detailed. **Validation** is inferred through **comparative analysis** between **simulated data** (e.g., Simulink models) and **measured data** (e.g., encoder-derived torque) to derive **friction coefficients**, aligning with **model-in-the-loop (MIL)** or **software-in-the-loop (SIL)** methodologies. **Uncertainty quantification** arises from sensor noise and model simplifications, but **formal error detection/correction mechanisms** (e.g., Kalman filters, reconciliation algorithms) are not explicitly described. **Quality assurance** processes rely on **ad-hoc validation** during the friction modeling phase (Sec. VII), lacking structured **cross-tool verification** (e.g., SysML ↔ Simulink consistency checks) or **anomaly detection** frameworks. The document emphasizes **purpose-driven aggregation** of data traces for Digital Shadows but does not elaborate on **systematic V&V protocols** for the Digital Twin itself.
"
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "The technical network infrastructure for the Digital Twin system is minimally described in the provided documents. The only explicitly mentioned communication protocol is **MQTT**, which is referenced in the context of streaming time-series data for digital shadows. No details on network topology (e.g., star, mesh), bandwidth requirements, latency specifications, redundancy mechanisms, or security protocols (e.g., TLS, VPN) are provided. The document does not discuss control flows, command execution, or bidirectional communication, aligning with the definition of a **passive digital shadow** (as opposed to an active Digital Twin)."
]

instance horizontal_integration : DTDFVocab:HoriIntegration [
    base:desc "Horizontal integration is facilitated through **heterogeneous tool interoperability**, where SysML models, ADLs (e.g., AADL, MontiArc), and simulation tools (e.g., Simulink) are combined via **shared data interfaces and connectors**. The system leverages **MQTT-based streaming protocols** to exchange time-series sensor data between physical devices (e.g., robotic controller *Siemens SINUMERIK*) and digital components. Interoperability is achieved through **standardized interfaces** (e.g., SysML IBDs defining component ports and connectors) and **general-purpose programming languages** (e.g., C++/Java) for cross-tool communication. Dependencies on external systems are limited to **manufacturing-specific controllers** (e.g., CNC systems) and **sensor networks**, with no explicit mention of enterprise systems (e.g., ERP, MES). Integration patterns are **tool-specific and ad-hoc**, relying on middleware (e.g., MQTT brokers) rather than standardized enterprise integration frameworks."
]

instance data_ownership_and_privacy : DTDFVocab:DataOwnershipPrivacy [
    base:desc "The provided documents do not explicitly describe data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, compliance with data protection regulations (e.g., GDPR), consent management, data anonymization techniques, or data sharing agreements. The Asset Administration Shell (AAS) framework (as referenced in [46], [47], [50]) includes submodels such as *PrivacyPolicy* and *ContractualCompliance* for theoretical governance, but no concrete implementations or operational details are provided in the context of Digital Twin systems or Digital Shadows. Thus, **Not in Document** for this characteristic."
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system adheres to **industry standards and specifications** for interoperability and modeling:

1. **Modeling Standards**: Uses **SysML** (IEEE 1730) for **systems engineering** and **AADL** (ISO/IEC 24744) for **architecture description**, ensuring structured representation of components, connectors, and behavior.
2. **Interoperability Standards**: Implements **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** (per Abstract) for **industrial interoperability**, enabling standardized data exchange in manufacturing contexts. Data is streamed via **MQTT** (Section IV.A.4), a **lightweight protocol** for IoT/industrial communication.
3. **Certification Frameworks**: No explicit mention of **certification requirements** (e.g., ISO 23247 for Digital Twins) in the provided documents.
4. **Compliance Frameworks**: Relies on **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., **SMArDT**) for governance, though no formal compliance frameworks (e.g., IEC 62424) are referenced.
"
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system incorporates **safety-critical design constraints** explicitly defined for the robotic asset, as documented in [44]. The **safety cell** of the MABI robotic arm (Max-100 model) restricts the maximum working range to **[-90°, 90°]**, ensuring collision avoidance and operator safety within the defined operational envelope. While the documents do not detail broader **cybersecurity protocols** (e.g., encryption, authentication, or access control mechanisms), the **safety cell specification** represents a **fail-safe mechanism** embedded in the Digital Twin’s physical twin interface. No explicit mention of **threat mitigation strategies**, **risk assessment frameworks**, or **safety-critical system certifications** (e.g., ISO 13849, IEC 61508) is provided in the context documents."
]
